title: CIRI employee represents at U.S. international agility championships
summary: CIRI Record Services Supervisor Heidi Vania and her Shetland Sheepdog, Ice, debuted in their first international competition at the Agility World Championships Oct. 1-3 in Rieden, Germany, as members of the elite 12-member AKC/USA Agility World Team. title: Baby Za’nay
summary: CIRI shareholder Norman Oleson and his wife Marie Oleson of Sutton are proud to announce the birth of their great-granddaughter Za’nay Lynn, born on Aug. 20, weighing 7 pounds, 4 ounces. title: Baby Chandler
summary: CIRI shareholder Jessie Chandler, of Fairbanks, is proud to announce the birth of her daughter, Ayla Bryanne Chandler, who was born at 5:33 p.m. on June 7 weighing 7 pounds. title: Southcentral Foundation to open new Valley Native Primary Care Center
summary: Alaska Native and Native American residents living in the Matanuska-Susitna Valley and beyond will have access to a new $50 million medical clinic offering an expanded array of services in 2013. title: The CIRI Foundation issues fall awards
summary: The CIRI Foundation (TCF) is pleased to announce that approximately 300 original CIRI enrollees and direct lineal descendants received awards for the 2010 fall term, totaling more than $800,000 in awards. title: Carr wins October Stock Will prize
summary: Mary Beth Carr won the October Stock Will participation prize. title: CIRI Spotlight:  Lisa Dolchok
summary: In 1999, when Southcentral Foundation (SCF) opened the Anchorage Native Primary Care Center, a Traditional Healing Clinic was opened to offer time-honored Alaska Native healing methods alongside Western medicine. title: CIRI shareholder Natalie Wood returns from Iraq tour
summary: CIRI shareholder Natalie Wood, who was profiled in the January 2006 issue of the Raven’s Circle newsletter, returned from deployment in Iraq in August 2010. title: A word from the president:  Fire Island wind poised to help resolve energy shortage
summary: Anchorage Mayor Dan Sullivan recently asked city residents to practice cutting their energy consumption by turning down thermostats and shutting off lights because he is worried that utilities won’t be able to provide enough power when the weather gets cold. His plan is to have area residents voluntarily take action to reduce their energy consumption in hopes of avoiding rolling blackouts during the winter’s coldest, darkest days. Unfortunately, the mayor’s fears are well founded. Southcentral Alaska relies on...
